paper_title: RobustAD: Real-World Robustness in Industrial Anomaly Detection
task_description: Build a robust anomaly detection system that maintains performance under domain shifts. Given the RobustAD dataset (3 sub-datasets: PCB, metal parts, piled packets), you must: (1) implement a standard anomaly detection method (PatchCore), (2) train on the source domain (clean images) for each sub-dataset, (3) evaluate on multiple target domains with different shifts (lighting, pose, background clutter), (4) implement the Average Relative Drop metric that measures performance degradation across shifts, (5) implement a simple domain adaptation technique (e.g., input normalization or style transfer) to improve robustness, and (6) compare adapted vs non-adapted performance showing improvement in Average Relative Drop.
breakdown:
1. data-and-setup: Download RobustAD, load 3 sub-datasets.
2. model-development: Implement PatchCore anomaly detector; Train on source domain per sub-dataset; Add domain adaptation technique.
3. evaluation-and-reporting: Evaluate on all target domains; Implement Average Relative Drop; Compare adapted vs non-adapted.
rubric: PatchCore implemented; Training complete; Multi-target eval done; Avg Relative Drop computed; Domain adaptation added; Improvement shown
notes: Verify license/data access, compute requirements, and feasibility before contributor release.

paper_title: TGB-Seq: Temporal Graph Benchmark with Sequential Dynamics
tags: Top 5 Pilot; Graph; Temporal Link Prediction
task_description: Build a temporal link prediction system that captures sequential interaction patterns. Given the TGB-Seq benchmark (8 datasets), you must: (1) implement a temporal GNN (TGN or DyGFormer) that processes time-ordered edges, (2) add a sequential prediction head that models the order of interactions, not just their existence, (3) select 4 datasets (GoogleLocal, Yelp, ML25M, WikiLink) and split them temporally, (4) train the model predicting future links within a time window, (5) evaluate using MRR and Hits@10 metrics, and (6) compare against a static baseline (non-temporal GraphSAGE) to demonstrate the value of temporal modeling.
breakdown:
1. data-and-setup: Install TGB-Seq, load 4 datasets.
2. model-development: Implement TGN or DyGFormer; Add order-aware prediction; Split edges by timestamp; Train future link prediction.
3. evaluation-and-reporting: MRR + Hits@10; vs static GraphSAGE.
rubric: Temporal GNN implemented; Sequential head works; Temporal splits correct; Training converges; MRR/Hits computed; Static baseline comparison
notes: Verify license/data access, compute requirements, and feasibility before contributor release.

paper_title: TabM: Advancing Tabular Deep Learning With Parameter-Efficient Ensembling
tags: Top 5 Pilot; Tabular ML; Parameter-Efficient Ensembling
task_description: Implement a parameter-efficient ensemble for tabular deep learning. You must: (1) implement a shared-backbone multi-head architecture where multiple prediction heads share the same feature encoder, (2) train the ensemble end-to-end with a combined loss (average of all head losses), (3) compare against 3 baselines: single-head model, true ensemble (separate models), and a random forest, (4) evaluate on 8 classification/regression datasets measuring accuracy/RMSE and training time, (5) measure the parameter count and compute the efficiency ratio (performance gain / parameter increase), and (6) ablate the number of heads (1, 3, 5, 10) to find the optimal trade-off.
breakdown:
1. data-and-setup: Load 8 datasets from TabReD/OpenML.
2. model-development: Implement shared-backbone multi-head model; Train ensemble end-to-end; Train single-head, true ensemble, Random Forest; Test 1, 3, 5, 10 heads.
3. evaluation-and-reporting: Measure accuracy, RMSE, training time, parameter count; Efficiency ratio + optimal head count.
rubric: Multi-head architecture built; End-to-end training works; 3 baselines trained; All datasets evaluated; Parameter count measured; Head ablation done; Efficiency analysis
notes: Verify license/data access, compute requirements, and feasibility before contributor release.

paper_title: Roboflow100-VL: Multi-Domain Object Detection for VLMs
task_description: Build a few-shot object detection system using vision-language models across diverse domains. You must: (1) select 20 datasets from the RF100-VL benchmark covering at least 4 domains (e.g., flora/fauna, aerial, medical, industrial), (2) implement a few-shot detector using a VLM (e.g., GroundingDINO or YOLO-World) that takes text prompts and a few annotated examples, (3) for each dataset, provide 5 annotated examples as context and run detection on the test set, (4) compute mAP@50 and mAP@75 per dataset and domain, (5) compare against zero-shot performance (no examples) to measure few-shot gain, and (6) analyze cross-domain transfer: train on one domain, test on another, measure generalization.
breakdown:
1. data-and-setup: Download RF100-VL, select 20 datasets across 4+ domains.
2. model-development: Implement GroundingDINO/YOLO-World with few-shot support; Run zero-shot detection on all 20 datasets; Provide 5 examples per dataset, run detection; Compare vs zero-shot; Test domain transfer generalization.
3. evaluation-and-reporting: Compute mAP@50 and mAP@75 per dataset.
rubric: 20 datasets selected; VLM detector implemented; Zero-shot completed; Few-shot completed; mAP computed; Few-shot gain measured; Cross-domain analysis
notes: Verify license/data access, compute requirements, and feasibility before contributor release.

task_description: Build a system for programmatic video reasoning across diverse reasoning types. Given MORSE-500 (500 programmatically generated video clips), you must: (1) load a video-language model, (2) implement 6 reasoning evaluators: abstract reasoning (analogies), mathematical reasoning (counting, arithmetic), physical reasoning (gravity, collision), planning reasoning (goal decomposition), spatial reasoning (relative positions), and temporal reasoning (sequencing, causality), (3) run inference on all 500 videos with questions categorized by reasoning type, (4) evaluate accuracy per reasoning type, (5) implement programmatic video generation: use the provided generation scripts to create 50 additional test videos with controlled difficulty parameters, and (6) evaluate on generated videos to measure generalization to unseen configurations.
breakdown:
1. data-and-setup: Download MORSE-500, load video VLM.
2. model-development: Run on all 500 videos; Per-reasoning-type accuracy; Generate 50 new videos programmatically; Evaluate on generated videos.
3. evaluation-and-reporting: Implement reasoning type detection.
rubric: 6 evaluators work; Inference complete; Per-type accuracy; Video generation works; Generalization eval
notes: Verify license/data access, compute requirements, and feasibility before contributor release.
